title: Frequency-resolved decoherence spectroscopy of a semiconductor charge qubit coupled to a high-impedance resonator
authors: Ekaterina Al-Tavil, Wonjin Jang, David J. van Woerkom, Ville Maisi, Stefano Bosco, Jan A. Krzywda, Jeroen Danon, Christian Reichl, Werner Wegscheider, Thomas Ihn, Klaus Ensslin, Andreas Wallraff, Pasquale Scarlino
abstract: Superconducting resonators coupled to semiconductor quantum dots provide a powerful platform to investigate light-matter interaction and decoherence mechanisms in solid-state quantum systems. Here we study a hybrid circuit quantum electrodynamics architecture consisting of a GaAs double-quantum-dot charge qubit capacitively coupled to a high-impedance, frequency-tunable SQUID-array resonator. By tuning the qubit transition frequency over the range $ω_\mathrm{q}/2π\sim 3$-$6$ GHz, we perform frequency-resolved decoherence spectroscopy of the charge qubit across a broad energy window. Time-resolved measurements enable us to disentangle relaxation and pure dephasing processes and to identify distinct decoherence regimes as a function of qubit frequency. We find that at lower frequencies ($\leq 4.5$ GHz) dephasing dominates the qubit linewidth, whereas at higher frequencies energy relaxation becomes the leading contribution. The measured frequency dependence of the relaxation rate exhibits a cubic scaling, consistent with charge-qubit decay dominated by coupling to a piezoelectric phonon bath and providing frequency-resolved access to the corresponding phonon-induced spectral density. Our results show that hybrid semiconductor--superconducting circuits can serve as sensitive spectroscopic tools to probe microscopic decoherence mechanisms relevant for a wide range of hybrid quantum devices.
summary: The authors used a semiconductor charge qubit coupled to a tunable superconducting resonator to perform frequency-resolved decoherence spectroscopy. They successfully distinguished between pure dephasing and energy relaxation mechanisms across different operating frequencies. The key finding is the observation of a cubic scaling in the relaxation rate, providing direct spectroscopic evidence for coupling to the piezoelectric phonon bath in GaAs.
main_problem: The study investigates light-matter interaction and decoherence mechanisms within a solid-state quantum system to probe fundamental limits of coherence.
main_result: The research successfully characterized the frequency dependence of qubit decay, finding that dephasing dominates at low frequencies while energy relaxation dominates at high frequencies. Furthermore, they established a cubic scaling for the relaxation rate consistent with coupling to a piezoelectric phonon bath.
method: Frequency-resolved decoherence spectroscopy was performed by measuring the resonator's reflection magnitude ($|S_{11}|^2$) as the qubit frequency was tuned across a broad range using time-domain measurements (e.g., Ramsey, Hahn-echo).
model_or_system: The platform is a hybrid circuit quantum electrodynamics architecture: a GaAs double-quantum-dot charge qubit capacitively coupled to a high-impedance, frequency-tunable SQUID-array resonator.
key_observables: Decoherence rates ($\Gamma_1$, $\Gamma_2$), coupling strength ($g_0$), and the measured resonator reflection $|S_{11}|^2$ as a function of qubit detuning and drive frequency.
important_parameters: Qubit transition frequency ($\omega_q/2\pi \sim 3-6$ GHz), charge detuning ($\epsilon$), and the ratio of coupling to loss rates (Cooperativity $C$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on fitting measured spectra to master equation models, and certain scaling laws for resonator impedance are assumed when extracting intrinsic couplings.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate device schematics, charge stability diagrams showing qubit frequency tuning, spectroscopy plots showing avoided crossings (vacuum-Rabi splitting), and decay rate vs. frequency/detuning plots demonstrating the cubic phonon scaling.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from describing the physical setup and Hamiltonian to performing time-domain measurements to separate relaxation and dephasing. It then analyzes the frequency dependence of these rates, culminating in a quantitative determination of the coupling mechanism via phonon spectral density analysis.
why_interesting: This work provides an excellent experimental realization of open quantum system dynamics, allowing for the direct measurement and theoretical confirmation of dissipation channels (like phonon coupling) that limit coherence in solid-state qubits, which is central to modern AMO research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) False colored optical micrograph of the hybrid device. Al structures (light gray) are deposited on a GaAs/AlGaAs heterostructure (dark gray), to define the superconducting ground plane, and the fine gate structures utilized to form the double quantum dot (DQD). Au leads (yellow) connect the fine gates to the bonding pads (not shown in the figure). The Al/AlOx/Al SQUID array resonator (red) is galvanically connected to one of the fine gates, which is driven with the 50 Ω microwave feedline (green). (b) Schematic circuit diagram displaying the DQD source (S), and drain (D) ohmic contacts, and coupling capacitance to the resonator (CPL). Cc denotes the capacitance between the...

FIG. 2. (a) Charge stability diagram measurement. The resonator reflection magnitude |A11| is measured near an inter-dot transition, as a function of the voltages applied to the side barrier gates LSG (VLSG) and RSG (VRSG) (see Fig. 1(c)). Dashed black line corresponds to the calculated ωq/2π, and the dashed red line denotes ωr/2π, using the parameters extracted from the 2D fit of the spec- trum to a master equation model (see Appendix A5). (b) Linecut of the measured |S11|2 in (a) at ε = 0 (red arrows) revealing the vacuum-Rabi mode splitting. The red solid line is the numerical fit to a master equation model (see text) (c) |S11|2 measured as a function of ωd at ε = 0 with different val- ues of t/h, achieved by gate voltage tuning while maintaining the constant number of charges. ωr is accordingly tuned with Φm (see Fig. 1(e)), to be resonant...
FIG. 5. Resonator reflection |A11| measured as a function of the VLSG and VRSG (see Fig. 1(c)). The resonator frequency was tuned to ωr/2π ∼5.9 GHz by applying a finite magnetic flux Φm.

FIG. 6. External and internal loss rates, κext (red) and κint (green) as a function of (a) applied magnetic flux Φm/Φ0 and (b) resonator frequency ωr/2π, extracted from the circle fit of measured resonator spectrum (see Fig. 1(e)). The oscillations in κext reveal a periodic modulation with a characteristic fre- quency of approximately 221 MHz. The black dashed line in (b) represents a sinusoidal fit with a linear background.
